text: My name is Gerard Feeney and on behalf of my fellow authors I'd like to present our case of a robotic tamus, the first to be performed in Ireland for the management of an early rectal cancer.
The patient is a forty-five year old female who initially presented to the gastroenterology service with rectal bleeding.
The colonoscopy image shows a polyp located posteriorly at the second rectal valve, approximately eight centimeters from the anal verge.
The video shows a polyp with a diameter of thirty-five to forty millimeters located posteriorly at the second rectal valve, approximately eight centimeters from the anal verge.
A partial polypectomy was performed and the sample sent for histological analysis where it was found to be a tubulovilous adenoma with high-grade dysplasia and features suspicious remalignancy.
The patient was thus referred to the colorectal service for surgical management.
A staging MRI pelvis was performed, which noted a mid-rectal lesion with the inferior aspect located eight point five centimetres from the anal verge, suspicious for T-one or possibly T-two invasion.
No mesorectal lymphadenopathy was identified.
A CT scan of the thorax, abdomen and pelvis did not detect any evidence of potential metastatic disease.

After discussion at the gastrointestinal multidisciplinary meeting and further consultation with the patient, she was offered management by transanal minimally invasive surgery using the da Vinci XI dual console robotic system.
The goal of this procedure was the excision of this rectal polyp with adequate margins and to allow for accurate histopathological analysis.
There was a potential for the patient to require an anterior resection with total mesorectal excision in the future based on this analysis.
The risks and benefits of this approach were fully explained to the patient, allowing for a fully informed decision.
Preoperatively, the patient underwent full mechanical bowel prep.
Under general anaesthetic, the patient was positioned in the lithotomy position so that the lesion would be seen at the six o'clock position intraoperatively.
Three robotic arms are visible: the first arm holds a fenestrated bipolar grasper, the second arm operates the camera, and the third arm is equipped with monopolar scissors.
An additional assistant port, an eight-millimeter air seal port, is utilized for suction, irrigation, traction, and aiding in specimen extraction during the procedure.
The transanal port is shown, containing three ports for robotic arms and an additional air seal port for the bedside assistant.
The surgeon inserts the gel point path port following the placement of anally verting sutures, preparing the surgical site for robotic-assisted colorectal surgery.
The surgeon inserts the camera through the transanal port, revealing the lesion positioned at the six o'clock position within the colorectal region.
The surgeon inserts the appropriate instruments into the operative field through the transanal port, utilizing the robotic arms for precise manipulation.
The video shows the tattoo marks placed distal to the lesion by the gastroenterology service, visible on the intestinal tissue.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to demarcate the initial margins of resection around the identified lesion, visible within the transanal port setup.
The surgeon utilizes a resection margin of five millimeters around the identified lesion, ensuring precise removal of the affected tissue.
The surgeon begins the resection of the identified lesion, progressing from the caudad (tail) end towards the cephalad (head) direction within the operative field.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to mark the lateral resection margins, following the same steps as previously shown for the initial margins.
The surgeon uses graspers to apply counter-traction, exposing the tissue plane and ensuring dissection occurs within the correct anatomical layer.
The surgeon performs a full thickness excision, carefully maintaining hemostasis throughout the procedure.
The operative field is shown in a picture-in-picture view, with the assisting surgeon positioned at the foot of the operating table, providing assistance through the air seal port.
After excising the polyp, the surgeon places it in the proximal rectum and proceeds to repair the defect in the tissue.
The surgeon evaluates the defect created by the excision, deciding whether to leave it open or suture it closed, with visible options for closure or leaving the tissue exposed.
The surgeon withdraws the monopolar scissors and introduces a robotic needle holder into the operative field, preparing for suturing or repair of the defect.
The surgeon thoroughly washes all remaining instruments in preparation for wound closure, ensuring cleanliness and readiness for the final surgical step.
The assistant inserts a V-lock suture into the operative field, which is mounted on the robotic needle holder by the operating surgeon.
The surgeon places a V-lock suture at the proximal apex of the defect, running the needle through a loop at the end of the suture thread to secure it.
The surgeon secures the resulting knot snugly using the robotic needle holder, completing the suture closure of the defect.
The surgeon repairs the remainder of the defect using a continuous suturing technique, employing a robotic needle holder to guide the suture through the tissue.
Care must be taken not to provide too much tension to the barbed suture as this has been related to subsequent knot slippage and thus wound breakdown.
The operating surgeon must also be aware that barbed sutures have an elevated risk of wound infection as the barbs may provide anitis for bacteria.
The surgeon assesses the bowel lumen following primary repair, checking for any signs of stenosis and confirming that the lumen remains patent despite the large defect.
The assistant cuts the suture to an appropriate length and removes the suture needle from the surgical field.
After repairing the defect, the surgeon retrieves the specimen and brings it to the transanal port for extraction.
The assistant uses an atraumatic grasper to retrieve and extract the specimen from the surgical field, preparing it for fresh histopathological analysis.
The surgeon removes the robotic instruments and transanal ports under direct vision, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical area.
Postoperatively, the patient was well and discharged on the first postoperative day and a five-day course of prophylactic oral antibiotics.
She was well at her first outpatient follow-up appointment.
Final histology of the resected specimen demonstrated an invasive, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma
arising in a TVA with high-grade dysplasia PT-I with clear lateral margins, a deep margin of six millimeters, and no poor prognostic features.
The patient remains well at routine follow-up and is undergoing standard post-operative surveillance for rectal cancer.
A robotic approach offers many distinct advantages for TAMIS compared to the traditional laparoscopic approach.

The improved ergonomics provided by the robot allows the surgeon to perform more intricate dissection with greater ease within the confines of the narrow rectal lumen, which cannot be achieved by rigid laparoscopic instruments.

The endo-wrist movement also allows for improved intraluminal dexterity, which empowers the surgeon to perform intraluminal suturing with greater efficiency and precision compared to traditional laparoscopic tamas, where it can be frustrating and time-consuming.
A very challenging aspect of laparoscopic tamas is camera control, where the surgeon and assistant often struggle for space while trying to achieve optimal views.
The robotic platform overcomes this by offering a stable, magnified, surgeon-controlled
This also offers the benefit of freeing the assistant from challenging camerawork so they can better provide assistance via the additional air seal port.

These advantages, offered by a robotic approach, may allow lesions that previously would have been considered too challenging for traditional tamas to be considered for a trans-anal approach, allowing the patient to avoid the risk of morbidity and mortality associated with the more radical resection.
As this case was the first performed by our service, it has thus provided many pertinent learning points which we have put into practice for further procedures.
The operating time of the index procedure was prolonged, secondary to robot docking and robotic port placement.
Since this case we have performed a further four robotic TAMIS operations which have taken significantly less time to complete.
This is primarily due to more seamless docking of the robot and placement of relevant ports.
The surgeon inadvertently grasps the tumor during the procedure, highlighting a moment of unexpected tissue manipulation.
Handling of the tumour is not recommended as this risks tumour seeding and this has been taken into account in our future procedures.
The polyp should not be handled at all if possible.
The surgeon discusses options for wound closure, indicating that the defect can either be left open or sutured closed.
The surgeon irrigates the wound following resection, ensuring adequate hemostasis and confirming complete resection of the tissue.
In conclusion, here we have demonstrated our first experience of robotic thalamus, which was utilized to successfully manage a patient with early stage rectal cancer.
The greater versatility of the robotic platform may allow lesions that would be considered unsuitable for laparoscopic thalamus to be managed by local excision.

Thus, robotic thalamus is an important evolution in trans-anus surgery, which serves to allow patients to avoid the risks associated with more radical resection in early stage rectal cancers and benign lesions.
We have also reported some important learning points from our initial experience, which served to inform us to modify our subsequent practice accordingly.
